coredata.dc:title: Doped-carbon dots: Recent advances in their biosensing, bioimaging and therapy applications
coredata.dc:creator: Ansari, Legha
Hallaj, Shahin
Hallaj, Tooba
Amjadi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: As a fascinating class of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), doped-CDs are now sparked intense research interest, particularly in the diverse fields of biomedical applications due to their unique advantages, including low toxicity, physicochemical, photostability, excellent biocompatibility, and so on. In this review, we have summarized the most recent developments in the literature regarding the employment of doped-CDs for pharmaceutical and medical applications, which are published over approximately the past five years. Accordingly, we discuss the toxicity and optical properties of these nanomaterials. Beyond the presentation of successful examples of the application of these multifunctional nanoparticles in photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy, and antibacterial activity, we further highlight their application in the cellular labeling, dual imaging, and in vitro and in vivo bioimaging by use of fluorescent-, photoacoustic-, magnetic-, and computed tomography (CT)-imaging. The potency of doped-CDs was also described in the biosensing of ions, small molecules, and drugs in biological samples or inside the cells. Finally, the advantages, disadvantages, and common limitations of doped-CD technologies are reviewed, along with the future prospects in biomedical research. Therefore, this review provides a concise insight into the current developments and challenges in the field of doped-CDs, especially for biological and biomedical researchers.

head.ce:title: Doped-carbon dots: Recent advances in their biosensing, bioimaging and therapy applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a fascinating class of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), doped-CDs are now sparked intense research interest, particularly in the diverse fields of biomedical applications due to their unique advantages, including low toxicity, physicochemical, photostability, excellent biocompatibility, and so on. In this review, we have summarized the most recent developments in the literature regarding the employment of doped-CDs for pharmaceutical and medical applications, which are published over approximately the past five years. Accordingly, we discuss the toxicity and optical properties of these nanomaterials. Beyond the presentation of successful examples of the application of these multifunctional nanoparticles in photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy, and antibacterial activity, we further highlight their application in the cellular labeling, dual imaging, and in vitro and in vivo bioimaging by use of fluorescent-, photoacoustic-, magnetic-, and computed tomography (CT)-imaging. The potency of doped-CDs was also described in the biosensing of ions, small molecules, and drugs in biological samples or inside the cells. Finally, the advantages, disadvantages, and common limitations of doped-CD technologies are reviewed, along with the future prospects in biomedical research. Therefore, this review provides a concise insight into the current developments and challenges in the field of doped-CDs, especially for biological and biomedical researchers.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon dots (CDs) [1,2], polymer dots [3,4], carbon nitride quantum dots [5], and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [6], are of great interest in various fields such as biosensing, bioimaging, and biomedicine. CDs are quasi-spherical carbonaceous nanoparticles containing oxygen with sizes smaller than 10 nm, which were accidentally discovered during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [7].

GQDs are emissive graphene nanosheets with sizes below 100 nm, unlike graphene sheets which indicate no photoluminescence (PL). The origin of PL in GQDs has been attributed to the quantum confinement and edge effects. Although some different properties have been reported for CDs and GQDs, such as amorphous structure and graphene lattices inside CDs and GQDs, respectively [8,9], they indicated the same outstanding properties involving low toxicity, biocompatibility, photostability, water solubility, and simple preparation methods. These properties made them promising nanomaterials for widespread bio-applications. Even though semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and organic fluorescent dyes with unique photophysical properties and high quantum yield have been applied in the bioimaging and biosensing, some undesirable properties such as high toxicity, low- biocompatibility, poor water solubility, and low chemical inertness have limited their application in biological fields [10–13]. On the other hand, the low quantum yield (QY) of carbon-based QDs, including CDs and GQDs, can limit their applicability. Two main methods, which can overcome this limitation and control the properties of CDs, are doping them with various atomic impurities and surface functionalization. In the surface functionalization, the surface of CDs is modified with various functional molecules (such as organic/inorganic molecules, polymers, ions, metals, DNA, proteins, etc.) to tailor the properties of CDs. Up to now, several reviews have been reported about the functionalization and modification of CD surface in various fields [14–16]. In doping processes, atomic impurities are introduced into CDs to modulate their electronic structure. Diverse heteroatoms and metal elements such as N, S, P, B, Cl, Cu, Zn, Fe, etc., have been applied for doping CDs. Doping can alter their intrinsic properties via affecting the electronic structure. Since the photophysical properties of carbon-based QDs directly depend on the HOMO-LUMO energy gap, these properties can be adjusted by inserting various types and quantities of dopants. Moreover, the QY of these dots can be significantly enhanced by doping atomic impurities, so many studies have focused on introducing new dopants and doping processes over the last decade. As a result of these efforts, diverse single and co-doped CDs have been introduced, and their applicability has been widely developed in biosensing, bioimaging, and biomedicine. Concerning the publication of several reviews about the synthesis methods, applied precursors for synthesis, physicochemical properties, and a wide variety of applications of doped carbon-based QDs [15,17–19], this review will focus on the recent five years progresses in the biomedical application of doped-CDs. We summarize various heteroatom doped-CDs based on the number and kind of dopants, and then their synthesis methods, optical property, and toxicity are shortly described. Subsequently, the recent applications of doped-CDs in biomedicine, especially in cancer photothermal and photodynamic therapy, in vitro and in vivo bioimaging, and biosensing to detect ions, small molecules, and pharmaceutical compounds, are discussed.

Doped-CDs are carbogenic NPs with a size smaller than 10 nm in which atomic impurities, such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, boron, and so on, are introduced during the synthesis procedure to improve their optical, electrical, and chemical properties. Depended on the number of atomic impurities introduced in CDs' structure, they can be divided into single and multiple doped CDs.

The most popular heteroatom for the preparation of doped-CDs is nitrogen (N) which stems from the similarity of the atomic radius of N to C. N doping processes can intensify CDs' PL and extend their application via the creation of new defects on their surface. Because of excellent photophysical properties and bio-applicability, N-doped CDs (N-CDs) have indicated outstanding performance in various biomedical fields, including bioimaging, bioassay, etc. [20–23]. Boron (B), as the left neighbor of carbon in the periodic table, has been applied as an effective p-type dopant for carbon-based QDs. It has been reported that doping with B could create desirable electronic and optical properties in CDs [24]. The other reported dopant is phosphorus (P) which has been known as an n-type donor. Due to P's ability to create substitutional defects in the structure of carbon-based quantum dot, it is expectable that the photophysical properties of CDs could be altered and promoted by P doping [25,26]. Because of providing energy trap states by doped sulfur (S) in CDs structure, which can capture a photoexcited electron and avoid self-quenching, S doped carbon dots (S-CDs) have also attracted especial attention [27,28].

The other atoms applied as a dopant for alerting carbon-based QDs are halogens, including F, Cl, Br, and I (16). Due to the F substantial electron-withdrawing property, the photostability of F doped CDs improved. It can be attributed to the reduced π electron density of the aromatic structure. Even though the exact effect of Cl has not been understood yet, the QY of Cl doped CDs has been increased compared to CDs without Cl [31].

Also, there have been several reports on the doping metal atoms, such as Zn, Cu, Fe, Mn, Tb, Eu, Gd, and so on, into CDs (16–20). The metal atoms doping procedure, resulting in the band structure modulation of CDs, can improve the optical properties and create novel functionalities for the doped CDs. It was confirmed that the presence of valence electrons in the dopant metal atoms could create the new intermediate states in the middle of the bandgap of CDs, which facilitate the processes of charge transfer and electron-hole recombination. As a result of this phenomenon, the QY of CDs can be efficiently enhanced [33,34]. On the other hand, metal-doped CDs can combine the biocompatibility of CDs and (near infra-red) NIR absorption property of transition metal ions and extended the CDs' phototherapeutic applications [35,36].

In recent years, doping multiple heteroatoms has also attracted much attention due to the various benefits from each heteroatom. Moreover, the synergistic effect between various heteroatoms can create unique electronic structures [37]. It was asserted that due to the creation of more surface defects via introducing several hetero atoms into CDs, the non-radiative recombination can be reduced, which can improve their fluorescence intensity. On the other hand, CDs with a long wavelength and narrow emission bands can be achieved by doping several heteroatoms such as B, S, and N inside them. It can be attributed to the generation of more uniform and reduced bandgap in CDs due to multiple heteroatoms doping [38]. These desirable optical properties would make multiple doped-CDs exceptional choices for diverse areas of nanomedicine. Up to now, lots of co-doped CDs, including N, S co-doped-CDs [39,40], N, B co-doped-CDs [41], N, P co-doped-CDs [42,43], as well as several ternary doped-CDs such as B, N, S and B, N, P doped-CDs [38,44], have been reported and applied in biomedical fields.

The synthesis strategies of doped-CDs can be categorized into two main classes: top-down and bottom-up approaches [45]. In top-down methods, larger pieces of substances such as graphite, carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene, carbon soot, nano-diamonds, etc. have been applied as precursors of CDs and techniques such as thermal combustion, laser ablation, arc discharge, chemical, or electrochemical oxidation have been exploited to produce the desired nanodots by breaking down the precursors. In bottom-up strategy, simple and common treatment methods such as carbonization based on heating, hydro/solvothermal and microwave methods, as well as sonochemical method, and etc. have been utilized for synthesis of doped CDs from carbon sources such as citric acid, carbohydrates, biomaterials, and various heteroatom sources [46]. Each of these strategies has some merits and demerits which would be chosen based on the intended application. Some of the most widely used methods for preparation of doped CDs are discussed here (Fig. 1 ).

Hydrothermal method is the most appropriate synthetic route for producing fluorescent doped CDs due to its facility, low-cost, non-toxic, great efficiency, and eco-friendliness. In general, an aqueous/ non-aqueous solution of carbon- and dopant- sources is sealed in a Teflon‐equipped stainless steel autoclave and is reacted under high-temperature and –pressure for starting the carbonization process [47]. The temperature and time of reaction are two of the most important parameters, which directly influence the optical properties and QY of CDs [48]. On the other hand, the poor size control of the prepared CDs can limit the use of this method to some extent. Up to now, several single- and co-doped CDs such as Mn-doped CDs, I-doped CDs, Fe-doped CDs, N,P co-doped CDs, B,N co-doped CDs (B,N-CDs), and N,B,S-CDs have been prepared with this synthetic method [29,41,43,49–51]. Due to the use of harmful organic solvent, the solvothermal method is more dangerous at high temperature compared to hydrothermal synthesis. Besides, special considerations are required for excess organic solvent after the synthesis.

It has been clear that the microwaves consist of long-wavelength electromagnetic waves which excite molecules (in general, polar molecules). These molecules begin to rotate and their thermal energy is increased [52]. Due to the ease of operation and short time of reaction, the microwave-assisted method is a popular method for chemical syntheses. As the microwave radiations interact with carbon-based materials easily, this method is also used for preparation of CDs and doped- CDs. It has been verified that a constant and high pressure of the reaction container is the golden point in this synthetic route for control of the size distribution and PL properties of CDs [53]. It has been also reported that the parameters such as raw material ratio, reaction time and temperature play a critical role in the optical properties of the doped-CDs. Similar to other doped-CD’s synthesis methods, the presence of dopants sources in the synthesis process can effectively improve the QY. For example, Das et al. prepared Mg,N-CDs with a very high quantum yield of 54.16 % by heating the mixture of tamarind extract, Mg(CH3COO)2, and arginine for 7 min in a microwave oven (750 W) [54].

As well as the microwave-assisted method, the ultrasonic waves-assisted strategy is another high-energy technique for the preparation of CDs and doped-CDs. In this technique, the mixture of carbon and dopant precursors in a desired solvent is exposed to ultrasonic waves for appropriate time. In recent attempts to develop new doped-CDs, in 2020 Gao et al. synthesized N,S,P co-doped CDs (N,S,P-CDs) using a facile sonochemical assisted route in a basic condition at room temperature and successfully employed it for the detection of tetracycline in milk [55]. In the other work, Kumar et al. prepared gallium doped CDs (Ga-CDs) via a sonochemical approach which shoved the antimicrobial activity [56].

For the preparation of CDs via a thermal combustion method, in the first step, the thermal combustion of various sources such as burning candles, natural gas, paraffin oil, and corn stalk produces carbon, which in the second step is subjected to treatment with oxidants (such as HNO3) to form fluorescent CDs [57–61]. The very low QY (below 3%) of the synthesized CDs and their aggregation are the biggest challenges in the thermal combustion strategy [18]. In 2019, Cheng and coworkers synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N,S-CDs) with a QY of 13.3 % by combustion of cellulose-based waste of willow catkin. These N,S-CDs with diameters of ∼7.3 nm have used for selective and sensitive detection of intracellular Fe3+ ions [62].

The electrochemical method is a type of “top-down” method initiated from different carbon materials such as graphite, graphene, carbon nanotube, and carbon fiber [63,64]. In general, two carbon-based electrodes which are dunked in an aqueous electrolyte are defected by applying redox potential and CDs are formed [65]. Low-cost, absence of very hazardous chemicals, and ease of use are some merits of this approach [63,64,66–68]. The main obstacle in this method is the difficult control of CDs’ size. In this method, the size distribution and optical properties of prepared CDs are extremely controlled by the potential and oxidation temperature [69]. From the point of view of applied temperature, the higher temperature generally leads to smaller CDs [70]. From the point of view of applied potential, Bao et al. developed size-controlled CDs by adjusting the applied voltage. They observed that the enhancement of the voltage can lead to the monodispersed CDs. As well, the CDs with narrow size distribution were prepared by applying different potentials. They observed that the average diameter of the CDs is reduced from 3.3 to 2.2 nm when the potential is increased from 0.5 to 2.5 V [71]. Also, the electrochemical method was used for the synthesis of P doped-GQDs. In this synthesis sodium phytate (C6H6Na12O24P6), a kind of food antioxidant additive, was utilized as an electrolysis medium and a phosphorus source. The obtained P-GQDs were applied as excellent radical scavengers for ˙OH and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) [72].

The most well-known and fascinating property of carbon-based QDs is their PL, although its exact mechanism has remained debated. Various mechanisms have been proposed for PL of these dots, including quantum confinement effect, surface defects, molecule-like states, and even synergistic effects of these PL centers (Fig. 2 ). The PL originated from the carbon-core can be attributed to the recombination of photo- generated hole and electron between quantized energy states, produced as a result of the quantum confinement effect. It is worth mentioning that the quantum confinement effect in CDs is due to the smaller particle size compared to the exciton Bohr radius. Up to now, several research groups have indicated that the bandgap of carbon-core energy states increases with decreasing the size of CDs, resulting in a blue shift in emission wavelength of CDs [68,73]. However, the PL originated from surface energy traps of CDs has been considered as the more probable mechanism, and lots of efforts have been made to adjust the emission wavelength and improve the QY and decay lifetime of CDs via controlling their surface states. Various moieties on CDs' surface can generate different surface energy traps, which can act as emissive energy states and produce different color PL [74,75]. Although CDs usually exhibit excitation-dependent PL, an excitation-independent PL behavior has also been observed in some cases. The excitation-depended PL has been related to producing CDs with different sizes during the synthesis procedure and electron recombination from different energy traps on the surface of CDs. So, CDs with uniform size and surface states have been indicated the excitation-independent PL behavior. It has been confirmed that the PL property of CDs can be affected by their synthetic method and preparation condition, such as carbon source, kind of dopant, synthesis temperature, kind of solvent, pH, etc. [76]. Based on this fact, a wide variety of carbon-based QDs, with various PL property including QY, maximum emission wavelength, and decay time, have been introduced. Most of the reported CDs exhibit blue and green PL emission in the range of 400−550 nm; however, various red emissive CDs have also been reported [19,38,51]. It is worth mentioning that the red emissive CDs with higher QY and a longer lifetime are desirable in biomedical applications, especially for bioimaging [77].

One of the most important methods for improving the PL property of CDs is the doping process. During the doping procedure of CDs with hetero atoms, the new surface defects are generated in the structure of CDs and the number of energy traps is increased. As a result of this phenomenon, the bandgap of CDs decreases, and the non-radiative electron transfer becomes less probable, resulting in improvement of the QY of CDs [38,75]. Furthermore, the importance of doping elements has been studied in the PL lifetime of CDs. N atoms, as the most popular dopant, can usually cause a red-shift in the emission wavelength of CDs as well as an increase in the QY of fluorescence. It can be assigned to the surface energy states generated by doping the N atom, facilitating the radiative recombination and decreasing the probability of non-radiative recombination of excited electrons. On the other hand, electron-donating N-related functional groups such as amino groups can increase excited energy states' stability by increasing the conjugation degree of conjugated systems in CD structure. This phenomenon can enhance the electron transition from the ground to the excited states, so amino groups can indirectly contribute to the QY improvement of N-CDs [75]. Recently, the S atom has been applied as a dopant to increase the QY of CDs [28,78]. S atoms can modify the CDs' electronic structure and create new energy states which can capture photoexcited electron, resulting in high QY S-CDs. Zhang et al. [79] analyzed the electronic structures of undoped CDs, S-CDs, and N,S-CDs to explain the high QY of the red/orange dual-emissive N,S-CDs. The results confirm that S, or N, S-dopants can generate more electronic states, and create in-gap trap states acting as recombination centers. While the S element alone has been indicated a negligible effect on the emission wavelength of CDs, due to the synergistic effect between N and S atoms in CDs, a unique electronic structure can be produced. N and S can easily bind to C due to their similar atomic radius and the availability of five and six electrons in their valence band. Therefore, N and S elements can change the bandgap energy of CDs and increase the probability of electronic transition from the ground state to the excited state, resulting in notably increasing CDs' QY [75]. Dong et al. [77] investigated the effect of N and S co-doping in CDs and indicated the significant enhancement in the QY of S,N-CDs (73 %) compared to N-CDs (16.9 %) and CDs (5.3 %). In addition, the obtained lifetime of S,N-CDs (12.11 ns) was longer than that of CDs (7.45 ns). They confirmed that a new surface state appeared in CDs via the N doping process, and the co-doping of S atoms can noticeably increase the density of this state. Indeed, the S atom can eliminate the O atom's surface state and stabilize and increase the surface state of the N atom. In the other study, the effect of dopant electronegativity was examined on the emission wavelength of doped CDs. For this purpose, the emission wavelength shift of three different CDs, doped with N (more electronegative than C), S, and Se (less electronegative than C), were compared. The PL spectrum of S and Se doped-CDs red-shifted due to S and Se's electron-donating property, while the PL spectrum of N-CDs blue shifted. Compared to non-doped CDs, a red-shift in doped-CDs' emission wavelength has been frequently reported, and a blue shift has also been observed in some cases [80,81]. Barman et al. investigated the effect of n-type (P) and p-type (B) dopants on the photophysical properties of N-CDs. They revealed that n-type doping (P doping) into CDs could increase the radiative relaxation pathway due to the incorporation of the excess electron, resulting in N,P-CDs with higher decay time and QY. While p-type doping (B doping) into CDs can enhance the non-radiative relaxation because of incorporation of the excess hole, leading to a decrease in the decay time and QY of obtained N,B-CDs [37].

Currently, metal elements have been widely applied to improve the optical properties of CDs. The metal dopant can improve the PL QY via the effect of their valence electrons on the electron transfer process, facilitating the radiative recombination of electrons and holes, and preservation of functional groups (such as carboxyl or carbonyl groups) on the surface of the CDs [34,82,83]. On the other hand, doping CDs with lanthanide elements, Ln-CDs, has been gain attention to producing CDs with the NIR emission and long lifetime. Wu et al. [84] utilized lanthanide ions such as Yb3+ and Nd3+ as a dopant to prepare Yb-CDs or Nd-CDs, which indicated emission at the wide range of 300 to >1000 nm. The reported CDs exhibited the excitation-dependent PL behavior of CDs at the visible region and indicated Yb3+ or Nd3+ related peaks at NIR wavelengths, about 998 and 1068 nm, respectively. They indicated that CDs could act as an antenna for the PL of lanthanide ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanisms of multi-color emission of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: One of the important PL properties of CDs, which extend their applications in diverse fields, especially in cell imaging, is the multi-color emission. This property lets researchers easily choose the excitation or/and emission wavelengths in the observation process. It has been reported that multi-color CDs can be achieved by varying excitation wavelength as well as by adjusting the influential factors in the synthesis of CDs such as temperature, amount of carbonization, the concentration of reagents, doped heteroatoms, kind of solvent, and so on. However, in multi-color emissive CDs, achieved by varying excitation wavelength, the color change of emissions is usually accompanied with considerable intensity variations. It is desirable to achieve the tunable full-color emissive CDs with the same intensities at the various excitation wavelength for practical purposes. Therefore, various research groups have focused on the preparation of these kinds of CDs and the studied the multi-color emission mechanisms. They have indicated that both carbon cores and surface states can be effective in CDs' multi-color emission. Several full-color luminescence N-CDs have been prepared using urea and citric acid and applied for investigation of the emitting mechanism by different researchers. For instance, Wang et al. [85] reported N-CDs with tunable full-color luminescence (from 400 to 700 nm). They studied the surface state related to functional groups of N-CDs and indicated that O-related defect states (C = O) and N-related defect states (C = N) could be responsible for blue/green and red fluorescence emissions, respectively. Sun et al. [86] have also prepared N-CDs by the constant mass ratio of urea and citric acid but different reactant concentrations in the solvent. They observed a red-shift in the emission peak of N-CDs with the reactant concentrations increasing. The morphology, structure, and spectroscopic characterizations of prepared CDs were studied and revealed that the enlarged particle size and increased C = O/C = N related surface states can impact the emission color of the prepared CDs. Based on the obtained results, they confirmed the importance of both cores and surface states in the mechanism of multi-color emission of N-CDs. In the other work, Hu et al. [87] synthesized multi-color CDs (6 different color-emitting CDs) from citric acid by controlling the temperature (at the range of 120–180 C). They investigated the effect of temperature on the surface states and emission wavelength of CDs. The surface functionalization increase with increasing the temperature, resulting in a red-shift of the emission wavelength and enhancement of QY. This property might be attributed to the functional groups on the surface of CDs. Despite many studies in the field of multi-color emitting doped-CDs, the preparation strategies are generally complicated, and the regulable multi-color emission is difficult to cover the full visible spectrum. Therefore, great efforts should still be invested to find cost effective and ecofriendly ways for the preparation of multi-color fluorescent doped-CDs [75].

Producing large quantities of CDs and doped-CDs in the laboratory leads to the release of a large number of nanomaterials, with unclear fate, into the environment. This could cause some adverse effects on the eco-environment, biological communities, and the physiological environment. A few studies have been performed to explore the impacts of CDs and their doped derivatives on organisms and the physiological environment. Thus, it seems that much more attention has to be put on this area of research. In this regard, Xiao et al. in 2016 assessed the toxicity effects of six kinds of CDs and QDs (S,N-CDs, N-CDs, no doped CDs, CdS QDs, CdTe QDs, CuInS2/ZnS QDs) on C. Pyrenoidosa (as a model organism). They determined different toxicity parameters, including the algal growth inhibition, Chlorophyll content, protein contents, acute toxicity tests (EC50), the activity of enzymatic and metabolites contents. The results show that the toxicity order of these QDs was: CuInS2/ZnS QDs < no doped CDs < N-CDs << S,N-CDs < CdS QDs < CdTe QDs. In general, it can be concluded that the toxicity of CDs in comparison with metal QDs was negligible, but interestingly, the toxicity of CuInS2/ZnS QDs was minimum. These deep toxicity studies are essential for further practical applications of carbon and metal QDs [88]. From the perspective of safety, the interaction of N-CDs with human serum albumin (HSA) was evaluated in the simulated physiological environment. The surface of N-CDs was decorated with carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups. The experimental data exhibit that N-CDs could quench the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA via the formation of a complex. Hydrophobic forces and hydrogen bonds drove the spontaneous complex formation. The results revealed that in the presence of N-CDs, the HSA was undergoing the secondary-structure changes by unfolding the polypeptides and changing the hydrophobicity of the region around tryptophan and tyrosine (Tyr) in HSA. This study suggests that the as-prepared N-CDs' biomedical uses may disturb the normal functions of proteins to some extent [21].

Lee and coworkers in 2016 studied the in vivo toxicity of N-CDs in the mouse model. The mice's hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, and kidneys were evaluated during the 30-day study duration post-i.v injection. Due to the high rate of N-CDs renal clearance, no obvious organ destruction or inflammatory reaction was seen, similar to the control group's results. For a better investigation of the potential toxicology of N-CDs, the in vivo test was organized. For this purpose, white Balb/c mice injected with N-CDs were subjected to the serum biochemistry assay and complete blood panel test on days 7 and 30. The results indicated that N-CDs were biocompatible for at least 30-days of study [20]. Although these studies indicate low toxicity of CDs, a general conclusion is impossible with these few studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of doped CDs in nanomedicine

ce:section.ce:para: The doped-CDs are widely used in different treatment technologies such as photothermal therapy (PTT), photodynamic therapy (PDT), pro-inflammatory activity, and antimicrobial activities.

PTT is a minimally invasive and potentially effective therapeutic approach attributed to the conversion of light energy (usually in the NIR region) into heat - using photosensitive agents to do some damage in the region of interest. PTT enjoyed many merits over conventional surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy; therefore, it sparked intensive research interest in the field of tumor treatments. However, the success of nanomaterials as thermal therapeutic agents in PTT is still doubtful because of the high preparation cost and complexity in their synthesis. Recently CDs and doped-CDs have been attracted much interest by researchers for PTT applications due to their smooth and cost-effective production, easy surface functionalization, excellent biocompatibility, and ability to convert the absorbed light into heat account of a large number of π electrons.

A significant aim of medicine is the achievement of systems that can run multiple applications at the same time. With the emersion of nanotechnology, this goal seems more and more achievable. To this end, researchers are being made wide variety efforts to develop novel multifunctional nanosystems that have two or more different functions at the same time, such as imaging, diagnosis, therapy, and drug delivery.

In the work by Yao et al., the versatile system which acts as imaging, targeting, and therapy agent was developed. Firstly, doxorubicin (DOX) loaded apoferritin (AFn) nanocages were labeled with Gd-CDs for both cancer therapy and bimodal imaging (optical and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)). Moreover, for achieving efficient, targeted delivery, Folic acid (FA) was attached to the outside of AFn. The as-obtained system's capability (Gd-CDs/AFn (DOX)/FA) in cancer therapy was assessed by applying the tumor-bearing mice during a 14 days’ study. The results show that the tumor growth could be gradually inhibited by Gd-CDs/AFn (DOX)/FA compared with the clinically approved DOX at the same dose. The pharmacokinetics study results showed that the bioavailability of DOX was markedly increased in vivo using Gd-CDs/AFn (DOX)/FA. Besides, it is observed that the half-life and circulation time of DOX in the blood is much prolonged. Furthermore, the in vivo MRI results suggested that the system above has the ability to take action as a proper negative (T1) contrast agent for MRI application [32]. In the other work, the multifunctional S, N, and Gd tri-element doped CDs (Gd,N,S-CDs) were developed during 10 min by use of a microwave-assisted method for a one-pot approach. These Gd,N,S-CDs with excellent fluorescent and magnetic properties were bounded to folic acid (FA-Gd,N,S-CDs) for targeted drug delivery as well as fluorescent/MRI. FA-Gd,N,S-CDs was able to enhance the MR response compared to the commercial contrast agent, Gd-DTPA. The in vitro MR and fluorescence dual-modality imaging confirmed the targeting capability of FA-Gd,N,S-CDs. Additionally, DOX was loaded into the FA-Gd,N,S-CDs with a loading capacity of about 80 %. This amount of drug loading is one of the most considerable amounts recorded for a delivery nano vehicle based on CDs. The in vitro results using HeLa cells showed the cytoplasmic and nuclear accumulation of FA-Gd,N,S-CDs and the nuclear localization of DOX. It was suggested that DOX reaches the cells by means of FA-Gd,N,S-CDs-DOX and subsequently detaches from FA-Gd,N,S-CDs-DOX due to the slight acidity of the lysosomes and further entered into the nucleus. The in vivo biodistribution study using adult zebrafishes that were exposed to FA-Gd,N,S-CDs showed the intense blue fluorescence that revealed the distribution of CDs all over the body. On the other hand, the fast clearance of FA-Gd,N,S@CDs would reduce the risk of prolonged toxicity and increase the biocompatible characteristics [89]. The other Gd-CDs sensor was designed by Du et al. for potential applications in MRI. This nano-platform showed a relatively long circulation time (∼6 h) following the tail vein injection of BALB/c mice. As well, the sensor is able to target tumors passively efficiently. Significantly, Gd-CDs were accumulated in the kidney and then were cleared simply from the bladder. The results indicate that Gd-CDs are suitable MRI contrast agents and radiosensitizers that can further be used as theranostic agents [90].

Photoacoustic imaging (PAI) is a relatively new area in nanoscience which is widely considered in designing multifunctional NPs. PAI, which can resolve the challenge of deep imaging, has attracted tremendous attention due to the non-invasive imaging. This technology is based on the delivery of light energy that is absorbed by optical absorption contrast within tissues and the subsequent generation of sound waves that are recognized by the transducer and resulted in deep-tissue imaging. Compared to other imaging methods using contrast agents with fluorescence emission, which usually can be imaged with spatial resolution and micrometer or sub-micrometer-depth imaging, the laser-based PAI takes advantage of photon conversion into the ultrasonic wave in biological samples. Since the acoustic wave can travel much longer distances through the tissue rather than a photon, PAI systems can circumvent the deep optical imaging limitations [91–93]. Lee and coworkers in 2016 developed multifunctional N-CDs with the ability of PAI and PTT. When N-CDs were injected subcutaneously into the left leg of female Balb/c nu/nu mice, the full-body PAI was performed for the determination of N-CDs distribution and clearance. The enhancement of the PA signal amplitude of the bladder over time up to 7.5 h post-injection revealed that the removal of N-CDs from the body is mainly by renal clearance. These results show that N-CDs can be used for the diagnosis of urinary tract diseases. In addition, for the investigation of PTT capability of N-CDs, Balb/c nude xenograft HepG2 tumor model mice were intratumorally injected with N-CDs. A thermal camera was used for the temperature monitoring of the tumor area following the irradiation of the tumor region with a NIR laser for 10 min. NIR laser irradiation caused the increase of the tumor surface temperature from ∼27 °C to 50 °C that results in the kill of cancer cells via hyperthermia. Moreover, the monitoring of the tumor growth rates showed that the tumor subjected to N-CDs and NIR laser irradiation was totally disappeared. The hematoxylin and eosin staining of tumor tissue and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick end labeling assay 12 h after laser irradiation showed signs of extensive nuclear shrinkage and fragmentation as well as cellular apoptosis, which proved that the PTT was increased in vivo [20]. Yang et al. doped the Mn into their prepared N-CDs (Mn, N-CDs) to impart N-CDs the photothermal (PT) effect for cancer treatment as well as dual-mode imaging. These CDs, which have long excitation and emission wavelengths, have been applied for both fluorescence and PAI. Besides the strong fluorescence emission, Mn, N-CDs could generate enough hyperthermia when exposed to the 808 nm laser light for the efficient ablation of cancer cells. The in vitro results demonstrated the destroying of 4T1 cells through the photothermal effect of Mn, N-CDs induced by NIR light irradiation. The in vivo PA and fluorescence imaging of 4T1 tumor-bearing BALB/c mice with Mn, N-CDs indicated that Mn, N-CDs could accumulate in tumor tissues significantly. The photothermal images of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice indicated that the tumors treated with Mn-NCDs reached the highest temperature (50.1 °C) in comparison with non-doped N-CDs (43.0 °C), which is high enough temperature to ablating of tumor cells upon exposure to laser irradiation [49].

PDT, which is commonly used to treat various diseases, including cancer, is a photochemical reaction between light and photosensitizing agents. Indeed, a photosensitizing agent, after exciting at an appropriate wavelength, can transfer its energy to oxygen molecules. During the PDT processes, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) are generated which can abolish cancer cells and a wide range of pathogen types. Currently, carbon-based QDs have been gained interesting attention as photosensitizing agents for the generation of ROS in cancer cells and tumors due to their biocompatibility, photostability, resistance toward biological corrosion, and so on [30,94–96]. Ju et al. [97] al evaluated the applicability of N-GQD as a photosensitizing agent and nucleus‐targeted delivery system for enhanced cancer therapy. Concerning the ROS generation property of graphene, they suspected that N-GQD could be applied as a photosensitizer and increase the generation of ROS, resulting in the destruction of cancer cells. They confirmed that the ROS generation performance of N-GQDs could be tuned by altering the defects and functional groups of N-GQD surface (via changing the amount of nitrogen source applied in the preparation of N-GQDs). Furthermore, N-GQD-DOX-APTES was utilized as a nucleus-targeted delivery system for cancer therapy. In-vitro fluorescent images revealed that N-GQD performs the main function as a photosensitizer in the generation of ROS in live cells (Fig. 3 ). Due to the small size of N-GQD, these particles were uptaken by cells via the size-dependent effect, as well as indicated exceptional nucleus targeting capability because of the charge reversal property of APTES. Based on these facts, Ju et al. indicated that the N-GQD-DOX-APTES has the ability to provide a suitable approach to develop a therapeutic multifunctional NP for biomedical applications.

In the other work, Marković et al. [98] studied the prooxidant as well as antioxidant applicability of various carbon-based nanomaterials, including GQDs, graphene oxide (GO), CDs, and N-CDs in the absence and presence of a visible light source. The prooxidant effect refers to the ability to generate ROS. They applied various microscopy and spectroscopy techniques for the characterization of mentioned carbon-based nanomaterials and investigation of their capabilities as ROS generator or quencher. They reported CDs as the best free radical scavenger as well as the best generator of singlet oxygen under the irradiation of blue light. It means that CDs have both antioxidant and prooxidant effects. Furthermore, the photo-cytotoxicity of CDs was examined for three cellular lines, including a cell line derived from human cervix carcinoma Hep2c (HeLa), human rhabdomyosarcoma (RD), and fibroblast cell line from murine (L2OB). According to the reported results, the photo-cytotoxicity of all samples was relatively less than that of cis-diamminedichloroplatinum (cis-DPP). They indicated that the photodynamic activity of tested samples could be affected by the preparation method and doped elements.

The other doped-CD with antioxidant activity was developed by Dehvari et al. They fabricated Mn,N,S-CDs with dual-modal imaging: fluorescent and MR imaging. The results showed that the as-prepared CD could scavenge the DPPH radicals moderately. Furthermore, hyaluronic acid (HA) was conjugated to Mn,N,S-CDs to endow them with better biocompatibility as well as targeting ability. The in vitro experiments proved the targeting capability of CD44-overexpressing B16F1 cells by HA-conjugated Mn,N,S-CDs. The aforementioned probe was further bonded noncovalently to doxorubicin for the fabrication of the drug delivery system. Here, it has been claimed that DOX-loaded CDs decreased cell viability by 50 % in comparison with the free doxorubicin group [99].

In the other work, red emissive CDs were achieved by doping S and N heteroatoms in CDs. Due to the high PL QY of red emission (QY 29 %), the obtained S,N-CDs were successfully exploited as bioimaging and theranostic agent in the nanomedicine field. It was indicated that a theranostic agent could be formed by loading DOX onto S,N- CDs via π−π stacking. The prepared theranostic agent was utilized in chemotherapy for cancer cells and indicated that the S,N-CDs/Dox can efficiently destroy the cancer cells. In addition, the applicability of S,N-CDs/Dox was investigated for the in vivo treatment of mice bearing HepG2 cells. As can be seen in Fig. 4 A, with the intratumoral injection of S,N- CD-Dox, an intense PL emission was detected, which confirmed the S,N-CD potential for in vivo bioimaging. The PL intensity was proportional to the theranostic agent concentration. After 7 times injections at the center point of the tumor, both the weight and tumor size obviously decreased (Fig. 4B). Based on the obtained result, it can be concluded that the S,N-CDs are suitable for cancer therapy applications [39].

Additionally, Das and coworkers developed a N,S-CDs NPs and used them for in-vitro cancer cell labeling. Besides, they loaded capecitabine into aforementioned N,S-CDs for further drug delivery [100]. Wen et al. reported the preparation of multifunctional N, S-CDs, which could be applied as the folate sensor, in-vivo imaging, and drug delivery [101]. In the other study, N-CDs conjugated with Quinic acid, as a targeting agent, were applied as both nanocarriers for drug delivery and PL markers for in-vivo imaging. Due to outstanding PL psroperties and desirable tumor accumulation, these CDs have been introduced as appropriate agents for multifunctional therapy [102]. In 2018, the porphyrin-implanted CDs (N-CDs) were synthesized by Wu and coworkers and applied for ablation of tumor in mice bearing MDA-MB-231 breast cancer via PDT. As well, the as-prepared system with NIR absorption could act as a PAI agent [103].

In Yue’s work, in 2020, ruthenium-doped CDs (Ru-CDs) were prepared via a hydrothermal method by use of citric acid and 5-amino-1,10-phenanthroline ruthenium(II) complex (Ru-Aphen) as starting precursors. Their work revealed that Ru-CDs exhibit intense red emission as well as highly efficient generation of ROS in the HeLa cells. The high efficiency of Ru-CDs in ROS generation suggesting that Ru-CDs possess the capability of PDT [104].

Ayaz and coworkers [105] synthesized blue-emitting aluminum (Al) doped CDs (Al-CDs) and studied their applicability as adjuvants on the immune cells. For this purpose, the in vitro pro-inflammatory activity of the mammalian macrophages in the presence of Al-CDs was investigated. It should be mentioned that undoped CD could not be applied as adjuvants because they indicated mild anti-inflammatory activity and no immunostimulatory potential. In comparison, the prepared Al-CDs (with 20 % aluminum concentrations) could increase the pro-inflammatory potential of the macrophages based on the TNFα and IL1β cytokine production levels more than that of conventional aluminum salt (as a confirmed adjuvant). They indicated that prepared Al-CDs were non-cytotoxic and could be applied as safe and suitable adjuvant carriers. The mentioned nanocarriers were stable over three months at room temperature. The other interesting application reported for CDs is osteogenic activity. Bone loss is a usual medical drawback resulting from some bone-related diseases, such as trauma, osteoporosis, and infection. Wang and coworkers [106] reported the osteogenic effects of Zn2+ doped CDs (Zn-CDs) in vivo. Zn-CDs showed practical fluorescence imaging as well as inducing osteoblastic differentiation abilities. CDs will be quickly removed from the body after intravenous or local injection. Here, Zn-CD modified gelatin/hydroxyapatite nanofibrous scaffold was prepared and implanted into the rats' calvarial bone defect. The results showed various osteogenic effects and bone regeneration promotion in vivo without any side-effect on cell proliferation in prolonged stimulation. It is essential to mention that the volume of newly generated bone in the Zn-CD group was twice more remarkable than that in the control group in vivo.

In the past few decades, the antibiotic-resistance of many bacteria has dramatically increased. As well, the lack of entering the market of new antibiotics makes it necessary to apply nanotechnology for the development of chemicals with improved antimicrobial activity. The antimicrobial activity of CDs and their doped derivatives has recently attracted many interests due to their excellent properties. CDs possess multivalent interaction capability with microorganisms. In work by Kumar et al., gallium doped CDs (Ga-CDs) were prepared via a sonochemical approach in polyethylene glycol-400, which plays two roles: the medium of reaction and the carbon source. The antimicrobial activity of Ga-CDs against various strains of p. aeruginosa showed that Ga-CDs were much more useful than Ga nanoparticles or CDs without Ga. The antimicrobial activity of Ga-CDs is a function of the Ga-doping level. Like many doping processes in the synthesis of doped CDs, the Ga content approaches a limit when its content in solution increases above a certain amount [56]. In the other study on antibacterial activity of doped-CDs, Tammina et al. investigated the morphological changes of both the gram-positive pathogens (Staphylococcus aureus) gram-negative (Escherichia coli) in the presence of bluish-green emitting N,Zn-CDs via scanning electron microscopic images. It is necessary to mention that glucosamine and zinc acetate were used as precursors for CDs' preparation by a one-step microwave method. It indicated that Zn-doping improved the photoluminescence properties of CDs (QY of 74 %). The prepared N,Zn-CDs revealed acceptable anti-bactericidal activity under light conditions against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. As well, the CD above revealed the anti-bactericidal activity against Escherichia coli even under the dark condition.

Moreover, the assessment of the bactericidal activity mechanism of N,Zn-CDs was done. It is indicated that the bacterial cell wall could be attacked by generated holes, electrons, and superoxide radicals which were produced from interaction between O2 and N,Zn-CDs under irradiation, and then they might affect the cellular organs that result in the destruction of bacteria. The obtained results confirmed that the N,Zn-CDs can be used as desirable anti bactericidal purposes [107].

Tracking the cells in living organisms seems to be essential for non-invasive, real-time visualization of the biological processes, especially for cell-based therapeutics. These techniques allow desired cells to be separated from other cells. To now, diverse labeling agents have been used for the labeling and creation of images of cells. Concerning the inherent optical, magnetic, or acoustic attributes of these labeling agents, different imaging methods can be applied to detect the cells in living subjects at a high spatial and temporal resolution. These methods include fluorescent-, radio-, computed tomography (CT)-, acoustic-imaging, and MRI. In this regard, NPs endow with tremendous help as they can be used as contrast agents for biological MRI and biological PAI, probe for fluorescent imaging, etc. Many groups have made efforts to apply nanomaterials such as TiO2, QDs, gold NPs, CDs and, doped-CDs in cell imaging. The emission of different colors (blue, green, yellow and, red), and taking many advantages such as low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, suitable cell membrane permeability, and high photostability make the doped-CDs powerful tools for in vitro and in vivo imaging, including direct fluorescence imaging, targeted imaging, imaging of chemical species, and imaging of temperature. However, due to the weaker autofluorescence of biomatter in red wavelengths, red emissive doped-CDs are preferred for imaging in the biological environment. Herein, we overview some reported applications of doped-CDs in the in-vitro and in-vivo bioimaging. Given that the number of such studies is vast, we have summarized some of them in Table 1 .

The blue emitted MnOx-CDs, reported by Li et al. [108], indicated a reversible temperature-dependent fluorescent behavior due to rising non-radiative electron transition MnOx-CDs with the temperature increases. This property of MnOx-CDs was investigated in biological environments within 10–60 °C. For this purpose, the luminescence variation of MnOx-CDs-pretreated HepG2 cells at different temperatures (20, 30, and 40° C) was investigated by confocal microscopy imaging. The increase of blue luminescence in the MnOx-CDs incubated HepG2 cells can be observed by varying the temperature from 40 to 30 and 20 ° C (Fig. 5 ).

These CDs also indicated good water solubility, desirable biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity. Concerning the mentioned properties, MnOx-CDs can be exploited in biomedical areas such as bioimaging and PTT to treat cancer. The MnOx-CDs could also confine the migration of HepG2 cancer cells, which made them exciting candidates for adjuvant treatment of liver cancer.

It is reported that for fluorescent imaging of cancer cells, the Europium (Eu) and Terbium (Tb) co-doped CDs, which possess the dual-energy transfer effects from CDs and Tb ion to Eu ions, enhance the fluorescence of Eu 336.8-fold without any antenna molecules. This probe is promising to be applied as a red coloring agent for biological observations [109]. The other doped-CDs developed using a hydrothermal treatment from the citric acid in polyethylenimine (PEI) was used for real-time live-cell imaging and blood brain barrier (BBB) penetrating. The quantum yield of these N-CDs was as high as 51 %. The in vitro real-time imaging of live 293 T cells pretreated with N-CDs confirms that after 8 h of incubation, N-CDs could enter the nucleus, again suggesting that CDs are excellent candidates for nucleus imaging. Moreover, the cationic surface of the PEI-passivated N-CDs and small size (∼2.6 nm) enables them to cross the in vitro BBB model [22].

For evaluating the biodistribution of the NPs in the body, the in vivo imaging technique is commonly applied, particularly in live animals across various time points. in vivo imaging can give the overall estimation of NP accumulation in different organs. In this regard, the fluorescent NPs have been imaged frequently. For example, Kuo et al. used fluorescence imaging to assess the biodistribution of N-CDs in an animal model. In this study, the mice's fluorescence imaging was performed after the injection of S,N-CD solution via tail vein into mice for circulation of N-CDs in whole-body. 12 and 24 h after injection, the N-CDs' fluorescence signal was clearly detected in the lungs. Most of N-CDs were mainly gathered in the bladder and then were immediately extracted into the mice urine. The large N-CDs with a size of >30 nm were retained in the lungs. However, more research should be done in the animal models for the determination of the biodistribution and elimination of different types of doped-CDs with different shapes, sizes, and surface charges [110]. In the other study, yellow-fluorescent N-CDs (YN-CDs) were used in the in vivo imaging including nematodes and nude mice [111]. The results showed that the nematodes incubated with YN-CDs revealed brilliant yellow fluorescence. Further, in vivo imaging in nude mice, injected with YN-CDs into the right half hip, showed low interference of background fluorescence. The abovementioned results altogether suggested the excellent optical capabilities of YN-CDs for biomedical applications [112]. Das and coworkers employed pyrolysis-based synthetic method to generate green emissive S,N-CDs using garlic husk (a green precursor). They investigated the in vivo imaging capability of prepared S,N-CDs. Firstly, the adipose derived mesenchymal stem cells (ADMSCs) were treated with S,N-CDs. Afterward, S,N-CDs labeled ADMSCs were injected subcutaneously in the outer side of New Zealand white male rabbits' ear. The two-photon imaging revealed the right signal intensity, suggesting that these doped-CDs can function as a newfangled agent for fluorescence imaging in biomedical fields [113].

The dual-modality imaging system has an advantage over a single-modality one, since the creation of two types of images at the same time could circumvent the disadvantages of each single imaging modality [13]. Also, concerning the fact that a combination of two imaging methods can achieve comprehensive information about the target tissues, dual-modal imaging can be applied as a promising imaging method in cancer diagnosis. Zheng et al. reported on the establishment of gadolinium doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs) and their application as a multifunctional agent for fluorescence imaging and enhancing MRI contrast in cells. They synthesized Gd-CDs (with a QY of 57.78 %) using Gd-DTPA and L-arginine pre-sources. They also confirmed the low cytotoxicity of prepared Gd-CDs and their capability for penetration of cancer cells and escaped from the endosomes. Moreover, the reported Gd-CDs indicated the ability to apply for multi-color fluorescence cell imaging (at various excitation wavelengths) and showed a superior effect on the MR contrast compared with Gd-DTPA (as a commercial MR agent). Gd-CDs were also exploited for the MRI test of animals with satisfactory results. Lastly, the serum biochemistry analysis indicated that Gd-CDs induce low long-term toxicity. Thus, these doped-CDs can be applied as a special dual-modal imaging probe for enhanced MRI and fluorescence imaging [114].

The biocompatible iodine doped-CDs (I-CDs), which were conjugated with cetuximab, have been developed as targeted dual fluorescent/CT imaging probes for imaging of EGFR-overexpressing cancer cells. The iodine ions and cetuximab endow the probe with CT imaging performance and targeting capability, respectively. The simple hydrothermal method was used for the synthesis of I-CDs (with the QY of 18 %) by citric acid and iohexol. The in vitro bioimaging using a confocal laser scanning microscope show that the lung cancer cell lines with increased expression of EGFR in comparison with cell lines with low expression of EGFR exhibit strong fluorescence, which indicates that the cetuximab conjugated with I-CDs could specifically attach to the EGFR-overexpressing cancer cells via EGFR mediated endocytosis [29].

One of the most important properties of CDs is excitation-dependent emission which endows them with the multi-color emission that makes them capable of precise cell imaging. The novel bioimaging sensor, N-CDs, with carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen as main building blocks, was developed by the one-step hydrothermal approach and Phyllanthus acidus and aqueous use ammonia as the carbon and dopant sources, respectively. The fluorescence microscopy images of N-CD-treated C.albicans strains exhibited a strong fluorescence, whereas the untreated C.albicans cells exhibited no autofluorescence, suggesting that N-CDs were successfully internalized in the cytoplasm. As the N-CD has various functional groups such as hydroxyl and carbonyl, the nature of the probe is hydrophilic that aids the probe to be confined into cellular interiors. Similarly, the confocal images of N-CD-treated Clone 9 hepatocytes show that the cells emit multi-color fluorescence includes blue, green, and red, under different excitation wavelengths, which is a great advantage for this probe. Besides, the probe's aqueous solution can be used as a new kind of fluorescent ink, the favorite inks used for paper printing [115]. The other full-color light-emitting CD reported by Nasrin et al. is folic acid-conjugated ternary doped-CDs. Since CDs were prepared from thiamine pyrophosphate (ThPP), which is a derivative of vitamin B, the multi-color fluorescence was assigned to the intrinsic heteroatom of ThPP: P, S, and N. The prepared S, N, P-CDs were employed for targeted bioimaging and photosensitization of the human melanoma cancer cells. The uniform whole-cell images (including the nucleus) were obtained by folic acid conjugated CDs due to the improvement of penetration amount induced by the folate receptor. It can be attributed to the acceleration of endocytosis and other biological phenomena. Additionally, the applicability of folic acid conjugated CDs for discrimination of folate receptor positive cancer cells from normal cells in the same culture medium was confirmed by fluorescence imaging analysis. The introduced evidence by Nasrin et al. confirmed the exceptional potent of reported CDs for diagnosing melanoma and phototherapeutic applications [116].

Compared to one-photon probes, two-photon fluorescence (TPF) probes in which two NIR photons (700–900 nm) serve as the excitation wavelengths indicate several advantages, including reduced photodamage and greater tissue penetration depths (>70 nm) to overcome photo-bleaching and cellular auto-fluorescence [117,118]. This kind of probes could be used for deep tumor tissue imaging in living animals and biological applications. In the work by Yuan et al., the nitrogen-rich CDs, hydrothermally prepared from guanidine carbonate and citric acid, were developed as a TPF probe based on the donor-π-acceptor (D-π-A) strategy. The probe had a two-photon absorption cross-section of about 61200 GM units and a QY of 63 %, which had not been acquired by former carbon materials alone. The developed probe can be applied to visualize live cells and live liver tissues up to 440 μm depths [119].

The assessment of microbial viability is a crucial process for antibiotic development, food safety, and human health. The dye staining is used for the determination of the viability of microorganisms. Compared to conventional dye staining, the ﬂuorescent dye staining coupled with ﬂuorescence microscopy or ﬂow cytometry has become a common way to differentiate live/dead microorganisms. This method possesses many advantages such as simplicity, being fast, sensitivity, and being visible. Lin et al. prepared cheap, photostable, non-toxic, and eco-friendly N-CDs using the bacterial amino exopolysaccharide by hydrothermal carbonization. They applied the bacterial amino exopolysaccharide without any dopants agents in their synthetic method. The prepared N-CDs were utilized as a staining agent to assess the microbial viability. The confocal images of both live and dead S. aureus pre-incubated with N-CDs showed any ﬂuorescence emitted from the live cells. In contrast, strong ﬂuorescence was observed for dead cells, implicated that the dead cells were stained by N-CDs. Therefore, N-CDs can be used for discrimination between live/dead S. aureus cells. These N-CDs possess some merits, including emission of multi-color fluorescence, convenient staining process, and high sensitivity and selectivity [44].

A wide variety of assays has been designed based on the exciting properties of doped CDs to determine different targets such as ions, small molecules, and pharmaceutical compounds. Diverse detection methods, especially PL-based techniques, have been developed for this purpose. Generally, three strategies have been utilized for designing CD PL-based sensors. The first strategy is based on the direct interaction between CDs and analytes. The second design is based on CDs' conjugation with specific receptors that can selectively interact with a target analyte. The third sensing strategy is based on integrating CDs with other compounds such as NPs and ions as a sensing part. In this part of the review, various doped carbon-based QD sensors that have been applied for biological purposes were summarized in three parts, based on the kind of analytes, including ions, small molecules, and pharmaceuticals.

Wang et al. [139] have developed a fluorescence and resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) duple sensor based on N-CDs and aptamer for K+ determination. They synthesized N-CDs by microwave method from urea and trisodium citrate as nitrogen and carbon sources, respectively. The fluorescence and RRS peaks of CDs appeared at 450 nm and 375 nm, respectively (excitation wavelength = 375 nm). In the presence of aptamer (Apt), the fluorescence and RRS intensity of N-CDs was reduced due to the encapsulation of N-CDs by Apt. While adding K+, the fluorescence and RRS duple signal of N-CDs was improved. It was attributed to the interaction between K+ and Apt, which resulted in stable G-quadruplex and free N-CDs.

Consequently, a di-model molecular spectral method for the determination of K+ in the range of 0.008–0.27 μM with a detection limit of 0.3 nM was developed. The method was applied to analyze K+ in human serum samples. Kalaiyarasan et al. [140] reported a facile approach for analyzing Cu2+ and ascorbic acid (AA) by N-CDs prepared hydrothermally from melamine. The prepared N-CDs indicated superior optical properties, such as a pH-depended photoinduced electron transfer process. They designed a double naked-eye, colorimetric, and fluorometric sensor based on a complex formation between Cu2+ ions and N-CDs. Furthermore, they applied N-CDs and Cu2+ to develop a turn-off /on sensor to determine AA based on reducing the Cu2+ to Cu+ by AA, which resulted in the dissociation of formed Cu-N-CDs complex. Therefore, the absorbance and fluorescence signals of N-CDs were restored by adding AA. They could determine Cu2+ ions with a LOD of 3.8 nM by colorimetric detection and 93 nM by fluorometric detection. The LODs for AA were 3.3 μM and 80 nM using colorimetric and fluorometric methods, respectively. The reported methods were used for the detection of Cu2+ and AA in human serum samples. Another selective N-CD based probe was reported for the determination of Cu2+ in urine samples. N-CDs were prepared by pyrolysis of histidine and citric acid. This sensor exhibited a stable, bright blue fluorescence. The sensor's linear range and detection limit were 0.6–30 μM and 0.19 μM, respectively [141]. Cui et al. [142] were synthesized yellow emitting CDs. They applied the prepared CDs to design a dual-function sensing platform based on on–off–on for detection of Fe3+ and Inositol hexaphosphate (IP6) in the cells and food samples with satisfactory results. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched by adding Fe3+ due to the photoinduced electron transfer process. Since gradually adding IP6 could recover the fluorescence intensity they assigned the fluorescence recovery to a high affinity between IP6 and Fe3+, resulting from FeOP bond.

A green emissive N-CDs was synthesized from urea and glutathione by a simple hydrothermal method and exploited to design a turn-off-on fluorescence probe to detect ClO− and HSO3 − in living cells. The schematic illustration of the detection mechanism is indicated in Fig. 6 . In the presence of ClO−, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was reduced, which can be due to the damage of the surface passivation layer of N-CDs. While with adding HSO3 −, the fluorescence signal of N-CDs was restored as a result of attaching hydrogen sulfite groups to the surface of N-CDs. To characterize prepared N-CDs and further confirm the mentioned mechanism, FT-IR and XPS spectra of N-CDs were also studied in the presence of ClO- and HSO3- [143]·

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL based sensor for determination of small molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Naik et al. [144] applied a hydrothermal synthesis for the preparation of N-CDs from carbon powder and used the obtained dots to establish a naked eye fluorescent probe for the determination of dopamine. For this purpose, they prepared N-CDs-incorporated gel strips through a simple approach. In contrast to other biomolecules, the strip's color turned to dark brown with dipping in dopamine solution. The designed sensor was suitable for the qualitative detection of dopamine and could be applied for its quantitative determination by fluorescence spectroscopy. N-CDs' fluorescence was quenched in the presence of dopamine, which was attributed to the complex formation or electron transfer process. The developed probe, with a linear range 2 − 20 μg/mL and a detection limit of 1.97 μg/mL, was employed for the analysis of dopamine in the pharmaceutical samples.

The other N-CDs, used as an effective sensor for detecting dopamine in urine samples, were synthesized through the hydrothermal method by polyacrylamide as both carbon and nitrogen sources. The linear range and LOD of the prepared sensor were 0.1–200 μM and 0.05 μM, respectively. In the developed sensor, the transformation of dopamine into dopamine quinone under alkaline conditions leads to the quenching of the fluorescence emission of N-CDs [145]. Similarly, Fe-CD based fluorescence sensor was developed for dopamine assay in biological samples. Ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid salt and ferric nitrate were utilized respectively as a carbon and iron source to fabricate Fe-CDs by a hydrothermal carbonization method. In the Fe-CD based developed sensor, Fe acted as a bonding site that could form a complex with dopamine containing catechol groups, and CDs operated as fluorophores. In the presence of dopamine, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched. They assigned this quenching effect to quinone formation, which stemmed from the oxidation of dopamine by ambient O2. The coordination of dopamine to the surface of CDs provided a suitable distance between electron acceptors and CDs, resulting in high quenching efficiencies. The developed sensor was used to analyze dopamine in the range of 0.01–50 μM with a detection limit of 5 nM [50].

In the other study, Tabaraki et al. [146] developed a turn-off fluorometric sensor based on N-CDs for the determination of aspartic acid in human serum and sports supplement powder as real samples. Linear dynamic range and LOD for aspartic acid were 0.5–50 μM and 90 nM, respectively.

Hematin, which is a toxic substance, is obtained from the oxidation of haem, which is the main prosthetic group of hemoglobin and myoglobin. Due to hematin's hemolytic property, it can cause lysis of human cells, including red blood cells. As well, by generating ROS, it can mediate lipid peroxidation and subsequently cause membrane damage. Ju et al. [147] applied o-phenylenediamine in acidic conditions for the synthesis of red-emissive N-CDs. The obtained N-CDs exhibited a high QY (12.8 %) and pH-dependent emission. N-CDs were exploited to design a turn-off fluorescent sensor for the monitoring of hematin in human red cells. The developed probe indicated a linear response in the range of 0.4–32 μM with LOD of 0.18 μM.

Gong et al. used acid fuchsin for the synthesis of N,S-CDs by a hydrothermal method. The prepared yellow-emitting CDs were applied for fabrication of di-functional and label-free sensors to determine pH and Curcumin (Cur). The fluorescence intensity of CDs (at 543 nm) was increased linearly by decreasing pH value from 5.0–7.4, and the pKa of the N,S-CDs was obtained 6.06. Since the mentioned range of pH was near the pH value of neutral cytosolic, the laser scanning confocal imaging by N,S-CDs was applied for the detection of pH in HeLa cells. Furthermore, the prepared N,S-CDs were introduced as suitable choices for real-time monitoring of pH variations in live cells with slight autofluorescence. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs was declined by Cur, which can be attributed to the combination of electrostatic and hydrogen-bond interaction and inner filter effect (IFE). The linear range of the developed method for the determination of Cur was 0.5−20 μM with LOD of 81 nM, and it was employed for measurement of Cur in human urine and food samples with satisfactory results. Also, two other probes based on B,N-CDs [148] and the P,N, B-CDs [149] were developed for the determination of Cur. The blue emissive P,N, B-CDs (with QD of 21.95 %) were prepared using a simple hydrothermal method from ethylenediamine, H3PO4, and 4-aminophenyl boronic acid.

In the other study, a selective sensor based on S, N-CD fluorescence quenching was reported for monitoring nitric oxide (NO) in serum samples. S, N-CDs were prepared under microwave radiation from pyrolysis of citric acid, urea, and sodium thiosulfate. The selectivity of the developed sensor toward NO was attributed to the applied sulfur source, sodium thiosulfate. The sensor linear range for determination of NO was reported 0–60 μM, and the LOD was obtained to be 0.3 μM [150].

The various nanoflakes and nanosheets, including hexagonal cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH), manganese dioxide (MnO2), and V2O5 have been employed as a quencher, with CDs to establish biosensors for analysis of small molecules such as AA in biological samples. For example, Zhu et al. [23] prepared N-CDs and V2O5 nanobelts and applied them to develop an IFE based fluorescence method for the determination of AA and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence of N-CDs, which was quenched by V2O5 nanobelts as a result of the IFE, was restored due to reduction of V2O5 nanobelts by AA. The linear relationship was reported to assay AA in the range of 0.01–2.5 μM with LOD of 3 nM. Concerning the fact that ALP can hydrolyze ascorbyl-2-phosphate (AAP) into AA, the developed method was also employed to detect ALP in the range 0.1–30 U/L with LOD of 0.04 U/L. The reported probe was utilized for monitoring AA and ALP in human serum samples. Additionally, as-prepared N-CDs were exploited for fluorescence imaging of human breast cancer cells with desirable results.

The other interesting N-GQDs probe was designed to detect ALP activity based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from the probe to CoOOH nanosheets. On the one hand, the fluorescence of N-GQDs is quenched by CoOOH nanosheets. The produced AA from hydrolyzing AAP by ALP could reduce CoOOH to Co2+ ions and subsequently restore the fluorescence of N-GQDs. Using this probe, the ALP can be screened based on the enzymatic hydrolysis of AAP by ALP to generate AA that restored the fluorescence of the probe. The as-developed system indicated the linear range from 0.1–5 U/L with LOD of 0.07 U/L towards the diagnosis of ALP. The proposed assay was applied for detecting ALP in serum samples with high sensitivity and selectivity [151].

Amjadi et al. [152] reported a turn-on fluorescent probe based on S,N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheet to analyze uric acid in biological samples. They indicated that MnO2 nanosheets could quench the fluorescence of S,N-CDs due to IFE as well as other quenching mechanisms. In the presence of uric acid, the fluorescence of S,N-CDs was retrieved due to a reaction between uric acid and MnO2 nanosheets. The retrieved fluorescence signal was linearly proportional with the uric acid concentration in the range of 2.5–30 μM with a detection limit of 0.8 μM.

In the other study, Li et al. [108] reported on the synthesis of MnOx-CDs from manganese (III) acetylacetonate (Mn(III)(C5H7O2)3), as both carbon and metal sours, by a simple hydrothermal method. The prepared MnOx-CDs emitted blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 11.3 %. They applied the MnOx-CDs and Fe3+ to design a turn on-off-on fluorescent assay for biothiols. The assay mechanism was based on recovering the fluorescence intensity of MnOx-CDs, already quenched by Fe3+. Indeed, the fluorescence intensity of MnOx-CDs was diminished as a result of the complex formation between Fe3+ and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of MnOx-CDs. With the addition of biothiols, the fluorescence signal of MnOx-CDs could be restored as a result of the dissociation of MnOx-CDs-Fe3+ complexes by biothiols. The developed assay was used for the detection of biothiols in human serum, which confirmed the practicality of this assay in the medical field.

Also, various PL sensors have been reported for the detection of biothiols such as cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), and glutathione (GSH) in different cells and biological fluid. These sensors have been designed based on fluorescence quenching of doped-CDs such as N-CDs and S,N-CDs by some ions such as Fe3+ [153], Cu2+ [154] and Hg2+ [155]. The quenched fluorescence of CDs can be restored by biothiols. For example, Sen Liao et al. synthesized the N-CDs with QY of 21.7 %, by hydrothermal method using hexamethylenetetramine as the carbon and nitrogen source. This fluorescent probe was applied for the determination of Cu2+ and GSH in HeLa cells. The fluorescence of N-CDs would be significantly quenched by Cu2+ by the formation of N-CDs/Cu2+ and effectively recovered by GSH through the complexation of GSH with Cu2+. The proposed probe has monitored Cu2+ and GSH with LOD of 0.09 and 0.05 μM, respectively. The achieved linear range for Cu2+ and GSH was 0.1–40 μM and 0.1–30 μM, respectively. As well, the fluorescence intensity measured at various pH values demonstrated that the synthesized sensor could be applied as a pH sensor in the linear range of 2.87−7.24. N-CD-labeled HeLa cells' imaging showed intense green fluorescence at pH 7.0 and remarkably weaker fluorescence intensity at pH 4.0. In general, the pH sensitivity of N-CDs is ascribed to the reversible protonation and deprotonation of surface moieties of N-CDs.

The on-off-on fluorescence sensor based on red emissive N,B,S-CDs was developed for the assay of Ag+ and L-Cys in the biological fluid and living cells [51]. The red emissive N,B,S-CDs were prepared from 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid and 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid by a simple hydrothermal method. It was reported that in the presence of Ag+, the fluorescence of N,B,S-CDs was quenched (off), while, with introducing L-Cys to the mixture of N,B,S-CDs/Ag+, the fluorescence of N,B,S-CDs was retrieved (on) due to the interaction between Ag+ and L-Cys (Fig. 8 ). To investigate the ability of these N,B,S-CDs for intracellular determination of the amount of Ag+ and L-Cys, different concentrations of Ag+ and L-Cys were added into cells previously treaded with N,B,S-CDs. Using this N,B,S-CDs sensor, the limits of detection of 0.35 μM and 0.045 μM were obtained for Ag+ and L-Cys, respectively. It seems that doping three heteroatoms endows the CDs with red light emission that is a remarkable property for CDs for applications in bio-related areas.

Furthermore, several research groups designed sensors by various doped CDs and AuNPs based on a nanomaterial surface energy transfer (NSET) to determine biothiols. In all studies, the fluorescent signal of CDs, already quenched by NPs, could be restored as a result of the formation of Au-S between biothiols and AuNPs. Due to the same structure and property of biothiols, GSH, Cys, and Hcy, it is important to develop a discriminative method to detect them. Xiang et al. [156] introduced a fluorescence turn-on sensor for the determination of biothiols such as GSH, Cys, and Hcy based on NSET. They indicated the applicability of the introduced sensor for discrimination GSH from Cys and Hcy by pH adjustment. N,S-CDs and AgNPs, as energy donors and acceptors, were used for designing this sensor. In the introduced nanosensor, the fluorescence of S,N-CDs was turned off by AgNPs due to NEST process, while the fluorescence signal was recovered in the presence of biothiol due to their interaction with AgNPs. They observed that under neutral pH condition, the fluorescence response could be restored only in the presence of Cys and Hcy, resulting in the measurement of the whole amount of Cys and Hcy. However, at pH 3.0 the fluorescence signal recovered with addition of all biothiols, Cys, Hcy and GSH, therefore determination of total biothiol was possible owing to the same slopes and intercepts of their calibration curves. According to the mentioned facts, the amount of GSH could be measured individually and the developed sensor was successfully applied for the determination of GSH in human serum samples. In the other study, green emissive N,S-CDs, prepared from 3-aminothiophenol, and AuNPs were also applied for the development of NSET based sensor for the detection of GSH. The developed turn-on sensor, with the linear range of 3.8–415.1 μM and LOD of 0.21 μM was utilized to determine GSH in human serum samples [157]. Interestingly, the dual-readout (colorimetric and fluorometric) sensor for successful detection of biothiols in the serum samples was also designed by AuNPs and N-CDs, prepared through a hydrothermal method from glycine as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The LOD for L-Cys detection was reported as low as 20 nM [158].

A novel and sensitive N-CDs fluorescence probe was developed for the FA in human serum samples. Here, the probe prepared using a hydrothermal method with nitrilotriacetic acid was combined with magnetic solid-phase extraction. FA could quench N- CDs' fluorescence with high sensitivity and selectivity with LOD of 0.5 nM (S/N = 3). Hexanoic acid-coated Fe3O4 (Fe3O4@C6) was applied as a magnetic adsorbent for serum sample purification in the developed system.
