The various components such as proteins, peptides, and fats are involved in human serum samples that may interfere with the quenching effect of FA. As these interfering species are hydrophobic, they can easily combine with hydrophobic Fe3O4@C6 and then can be removed by the use of an external magnet so as to achieve purification. The purified sample was exposed to the fluorescent probe for FA determination. Because of the property of magnetic purification and high sensitive detection, the introduced method was successfully employed for the measurement of FA in human serum samples [159].

Doped CDs also have been applied to design nucleic acid-based sensors. In the study for G-quadruplex (the secondary DNA structure) detection, the researchers tried to assemble Hemin (an iron-containing porphyrin) on N-CDs in a non-covalent way to form quenched Hemin/CD conjugates. The High fluorescent N-CDs were synthesized by using a green hydrothermal process from Ganoderma lucidum (an oriental fungus). Hemin has an inherent high selectivity towards G-quadruplex; that is why Hemin is separated from N-CDs in the presence of G-quadruplex and preferably stabilizes G quadruplex that subsequently results in the restoring of the fluorescence of N-CDs. This is the first time a study has designed an approach for both visual and spectrophotometric detection of G-quadruplexes [160]. The other N-CDs, synthesized from waste seaweed Sargassum ﬂuitans as a carbon source, were used for detecting double-stranded DNA, single-stranded DNA, and RNA. Upon tagging N-CDs with nucleic acids, the ﬂuorescence of the sensor was increased signiﬁcantly [161].

α-glucosidase is an enzyme that hydrolyzes disaccharides into glucose. Nowadays, an α-glucosidase inhibitor is widely used as a treatment for diabetes. 4-Nitrophenyl-α-D- glucopyranoside, a substrate of α-glucosidase, produces 4-nitrophenol whose absorption overlaps with the excitation of N-CDs. So, the fluorescence of these N-CDs is turned off (due to IFE) by the enzymatic activity of α-glucosidase. The achieved detection limit was 0.01 U/mL (S/N = 3). As well, the sensor mentioned above was used successfully for the screening of α-glucosidase inhibitor, acarbose, with a detection limit of 10−8 M [162]. Nowadays, early diagnosis of tumor biomarkers would help the operative prevention and treatment of cancer. Lu et al. were developed an IFE based sensor by N-CDs for the determination of β-glucuronidase (GLU), as an aggressive tumor biomarker. The catalytic activity of GLU leads to the hydrolysis of 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-glucuronide to p-nitrophenol, which is an absorber in IFE that turns the fluorescence of N-CDs off. Accordingly, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was proportional to GLU activity. The LOD of 0.3 U L-1 and the linear relationship from 1 U L-1 to 60 U L-1 were achieved to determine the catalytic activity of α-glucosidase. In recent years, discovering novel inhibitors for tumor biomarkers has attracted great attention because of providing the possibility for the early diagnosis of the tumor and discovering the new anti-tumor drugs. In this regard, the aforementioned IFE method was successfully employed for the screening of GLU inhibitors from natural products [163].

A similar work also reported the detection of β-glucuronidase using two-photon emissive N-CDs. They also used this CD as a nanocarrier for visualization of the anticancer drug loading and releasing in HeLa cells via FRET in real-time. Also, N-CDs' ability was investigated for two-photon imaging and monitoring in deep tissues [164]. Concerning the improved PL propitious of doped CDs and their biocompatibility, the development of multifunctional CD base nanosystems for simultaneous diagnosis and treatment of various diseases have gained special attention recently. Das et al. investigated the applicability of B,S-CDs for diagnosis and treatment of blood sugar at the same time. B,S-CDs were applied to detect glucose based on their fluorescence quenching and were tested as carriers for loading the anti-diabetic drug, metformin [165].

Other N-CDs were synthesized using the microwave-assisted green method. The N-CDs were immobilized by an enzyme called quinolinate phosphoribosyl transferase (QPRTase) to form N-CDs-QPRTase as a selective fluorescent bioprobe for the detection of endogenous neurotoxin quinolinic acid (QA). The elevated serum level of QA is a biomarker of many diseases such as Alzheimer’s, Huntington’s, HIV associated dementia, and vitamin B6 deficiency. As this probe was applicable in the linear range of 3.22–51 μM with the detection limit of ∼ 6.51 μM, it can be employed for QA detection in serum samples for the early recognition of many neurological disorders as well as other diseases [166].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL based sensor for determination of pharmaceuticals
ce:section.ce:para: Concerning the tiny difference between efficiently therapeutic and toxic dosages of pharmaceutical compounds, it is important to establish effective assays for monitoring diverse pharmaceutical compounds in biological fluids. Various analytical methods including doped-CD based sensors, have been developed for the biological detection of drugs in trace amounts. For example, Jiang et al. [167] introduced a simple and green hydrothermal synthesis of N-CDs using Ginko leaves as a carbon source. These N-CDs indicated strong blue fluorescence with a 22.8 % QY. Furthermore, the label-free detection of salazosulfapyridine (SASP) was developed based on the strong quenching effect of SASP on the prepared N-CDs. The N-CDs quenching fluorescence mechanism was investigated and assigned to both IFE and static quenching. There was a good linear relationship between the SASP concentration and the quenched signal in the range of 0.1–80 M with a detection limit of 40 nM. The established method was applied to detect SASP in mouse plasma with satisfactory results.

Zhao et al. [168] developed an IFE based fluorescence probe for analysis of methotrexate by N,S-CDs. For this purpose, they applied a hydrothermal method for the preparation of S,N-CDs using citric acid and L-Cys as carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur sources. In this probe, methotrexate which acted as an absorber, could quench the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CDs. The linear range of 0.4–41.3 μg/mL with LOD of 12 ng/mL, was reported to determine methotrexate. Furthermore, the applicability of the developed method was investigated for the analysis of methotrexate in the spiked samples such as hospital waste and the extracellular fluids of A549 cells. As well, Zuo et al. [169] reported another N,S-CDs with QD 75 %, prepared from citric acid and glutathione, for sensitive monitoring of ferric ion, methotrexate and temperature. They claimed that N,S-CDs can be applied as a promising platform for designing multifunctional probes and used the mentioned probe for the assay of methotrexate in human plasma samples. In the other work, Zhou et al. [170] synthesized high QY (76.5 %) N,S‐CDs by citric acid as the carbon source and glutathione as nitrogen and sulfur donor by a microwave-assisted oil bath method. They applied N,S-CDs and AuNPs to develop a FRET-based sensor for the determination of methimazole.

The other off-on N-CDs - ferric ions (Fe+3) fluorescence sensor was designed for the analysis of trace levels of zoledronic acid (ZA) drug in human serum samples. N-CDs with a highly relative QY of 12.5 % were synthesized through an eco-friendly hydrothermal method using a date kernel as a precursor. The green fluorescence of N-CDs was remarkably declined due to the interaction between Fe+3 and the functional groups on the surface of N-CDs (off). In the presence of ZA, the fluorescence signal of the sensor would be restored (ON) due to the coordination of Fe+3 to ZA. There was a linear relationship between the concentration of ZA and the fluorescence intensity in the range of 0.1–10.0 μM with a limit of detection of 0.04 μM. Moreover, the desirable bioapplicability and cellular uptake of N-CDs make it a potential candidate for bioimaging applications [171].

Doped-CDs not only attracted tremendous consideration for developing PL based sensors but also widely applied for introducing interesting colorimetric, chemiluminescence (CL), electrochemiluminescence (ECL), electrochemical and photoelectrochemical sensors.

Recently, CDs have been applied to prepare metal nanocomposites, including Au and Ag nanocomposites which were exploited to develop colorimetric sensors for detecting various biological and pharmaceutical compounds in biological samples. Our research group also designed a colorimetric probe based on Au@N-CD nanocomposite and Ag+ for the determination of isoniazid in human plasma samples. We indicated that in the presence of isoniazid Ag shell could be formed on the surface of the Au@N-CDs, resulting in the color change from red to orange. The color alteration was proportional to isoniazid concentration in the range of 0.1–2.0 μM with a LOD (3 s) of 0.06 μM [172].

In the other work, S-CD modified AuNPs were exploited to establish a colorimetric probe for the analysis of dopamine. This sensor's detection mechanism was attributed to the formation of aggregated S-CDs@Au NPs due to the complex formation between carboxylic groups of S-CDs@Au NPs and amine groups of dopamine in the presence of Fe3+ ions as the linkers. As a result of this phenomenon, the solution color was altered from red to purple. The color alteration was proportional to the dopamine concentration. The established method was used for the monitoring of dopamine in pharmaceutical and biological samples [112].

Amjadi et al. [173] designed a colorimetric probe based on N-CDs and AgNPs to analyze thioridazine hydrochloride in biological fluids. They prepared N-CDs by a green hydrothermal method from lentils as the same precursors of carbon and nitrogen. They indicated the color change of AgNPs from yellow to light orange due to the adsorption of N-CDs on the surface of AgNPs. Then the color of N-CD/AgNP solution was altered to red by adding thioridazine hydrochloride. This interesting behavior was employed to establish a colorimetric probe to monitor thioridazine hydrochloride in the range of 5.0–150 μg/L with a LOD of 3.0 μg/L.

Although CL method has been known as a suitable approach in bioanalytical applications due to the simplicity of instrumentation, short time of analysis, high sensitivity, low LOD, and large linear ranges, the weak signal of CL reactions can limit their application in this field [174]. It is worth mentioning that doped CDs gain outstanding attention to intensify traditional weak CL reactions, which can extend the application of these reactions in biological assays [175,176]. Amjadi research group reported the substantial enhancing effect of Si-CDs and S,N-CDs on the several weak CL reactions, including K2S2O8-Fe2+, HCO3 −-H2O2 and luminol-Mn(IV), and applied the enhanced CL systems for designing sensitive CL sensors to detect some biological and pharmaceutical compounds such as norfloxacin, catecholamines and uric acid in human biological fluid [176–178]. They also developed the CL sensor for the high efficient detection of indomethacin in biological fluid based on direct CL of S,N-CDs induced by KMnO4. The S,N-CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and L-Cys. In the reported sensor, KMnO4 as an oxidant can interact with S, N-CDs to generate excited-state S, N-CDs*, which then return to the ground state with emitting of CL. In the presence of indomethacin due to the competition between this drug and S,N-CDs for KMnO4, the CL intensity of S,N-CD-KMnO4 significantly decreases. This probe's linear range for the detection of indomethacin was 0.1–1.5 mg/L and the LOD was 65 μg/L [47]. A similar system was used for the determination of trace amounts of Cu2+ in human plasma. Similarly, the CL intensity of S, N-CD-KMnO4 was remarkably diminished by Cu2+ ions. The linear range of this Cu2+ sensor was 0.01 – 0.5 mg/ L and the LOD was 2.1 μg/ L [40]. The other S, N-CDs sensor based on the CL method was developed to determine ranitidine in capsules. The linear range of 0.5−50 μg/mL and LOD of 0.12 μg/mL was achieved [179].

The newly designed DNA functionalized N-CDs was proposed for high sensitivity and selectivity determination of miRNA-21 based on ECL. As shown in Fig. 9 , the hairpin probe1 (HP1) which was linked to N-CDs, includes three different areas, a complimentary domain of the target miRNA-21, a complimentary domain of "assistant DNA" that takes action as the diagnostic sequence for the nicking enzyme Nb.BbvCI, and a complimentary domain of hairpin probe 2 (HP2). When the assistant DNA and the target miRNA-21 are treated with the HP1-N-CDs the Y junction structure is formed. The nicking enzyme Nb.BbvCI cleaves the Y junction and subsequently, the target miRNA-21 and the assistant DNA are released (Scheme 8, A). The released target miRNA-21 and assistant DNA are hybridized with other HP1-NCDs to promote the over and over recycling processes that amplify the signal (Fig. 9, Scheme B). On the other hand, GO/Au nanocomposites immobilize the HP2 and promote electric transmission. After completing the nicking enzyme-assisted signal amplification, the complementary domain of HP2 was later hybridized with HP2. The structure HP2 loop is cleaved to form dsDNA immobilized on the GO/Au nanocomposite. The NCDs intensified the ECL signal after the construction of the dsDNA structure. The improvement of ECL signal was proportional to the concentration of the target miRNA-21 in a linear range from 10 aM to104 fM with a LOD of 10 aM [180].

Beside a wide variety of doped-CDs optical biosensors, some electrochemical biosensors were also reported in which doped CDs and GQDs were employed to catalyze some electrochemical reactions and improve the signaling processes.

For example, the N-CD modified glassy carbon electrode was used for the detection of uric acid, allopurinol, and Tyr in the biological samples, simultaneously. N-CDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing of L-asparagine and directly attached to the glassy carbon electrode. Peroxynitrite is an oxidant species that induce cell death. Trapping the peroxynitrite by uric acid avoids the reaction between peroxynitrite and Tyr and subsequently prevents the inactivation of cellular enzymes. When uric acid decreases in the body, the body loses protection against peroxynitrite toxicity. However, allopurinol is administered in uric acid lowering therapy. Hence, it is necessary to control the amount of uric acid, allopurinol, and Tyr in human biological fluids. This method shows the LOD of 13 × 10−10 M and the linear range of 924 μA mM-1 cm-2 for the detection of uric acid [181]. In another study, a nanohybrid of functionalized N-GQDs and bimetallic Au/Ag core-shell was applied as an effective electron mediator to design the Au/AgNRs@N-GQDs-MIP modified screen printing carbon electrode (SPCE) sensor for analysis of an anticancer drug, hydroxyurea, in biological samples. The developed sensor indicated desirable selectivity and sensitivity with LODs, 0.06, 0.07, and 0.04 in urine and blood plasma samples, respectively [182]. Pang et al. [183] fabricated photoelectrochemical biosensors to determine pcDNA3-HBV by a modified electrode using TiO2 NPs/N-GQDs/g-C3N4 QDs as the photoactive materials. They indicated that with introducing N-GQDs and g-C3N4 QDs to TiO2, the electrical transport of carriers was increased and conversion efficiency was enhanced. The reported biosensor displayed a low detection limit of 0.005 fM and a linear range from 0.01–20 nM.

A photoelectrochemical biosensor for detecting fibroblast-like synoviocyte cell was developed using ZnO nanorods (NRs) as a photo-to-electron generator. However, ZnO suffers from a wide band gap of about 3.37 eV. Therefore, compositing with narrow band gap photosensitizer material is an effective strategy to promote the light-response of ZnO NRs. Here, N-CDs and CH3NH3PbI3 were applied as photosensitizers. The results indicated that after N-CDs were modified, the photocurrent response improved [184].

In summary, doping CDs with heteroatoms is an efficient way to improve the physicochemical properties of CDs via adjustment of their electronic structure, which extends their application in various areas. In the present review, we studied the different applications of doped-CDs in the field of pharmacy and medicine in which these nanomaterials have been applied as the detection, cell imaging, and therapeutic agents in both in vivo and in vitro models. We described the various heteroatom doped-CDs based on the number and kind of dopants and then discussed the synthesis methods, optical property, and toxicity of doped-CDs. Also, the recent applications of doped-CDs in biomedicine were introduced by giving some useful examples. Our survey in the currently reported doped-CDs indicated that the N-CDs are the most popular doped-CDs for biomedical applications. We also found the hydrothermal synthesis is the most reported preparation method for doped-CDs. As well, among various biomedical applications of doped-CDs, their application in bioimaging and optical sensing methods, especially PL-based methods, has been gained more attention. It should be mentioned that most reported doped-CDs have indicated the blue or green emission that can overlap with the background bioﬂuorescence in organisms. Doped-CDs with red emission have been introduced as outstanding candidates in biological applications such as sensing, target labeling, and cell imaging [38,39,185], due to their exceptional properties such as minimum fluorescence background, low photodamage, and deep penetration into biometrics. Doping CDs with three heteroatoms has been reported as one way to achieve PL in a near-infrared region [38,51]. Moreover, the optimization of synthesis conditions, including optimization of carbon source, temperature, and solvent type, indicate a great effect on shifting the emission wavelength to the longer wavelengths. However, the exact mechanism of red emission and band gap narrowing of CDs is still unclear, and no systematic method has been introduced for the preparation of red-emissive CDs.

After more than ten years' development, CDs and their doped derivatives have significantly grown as an outstanding fluorescent probe with unique advantages including easy and cost-effective synthesis/surface functionalization, adjustable composition, acceptable physicochemical stability and photostability, good solubility in aqueous solutions, low toxicity, outstanding biocompatibility, and environmental friendliness, when compared with organic dyes and classical semiconductor QDs [13]. Furthermore, several unique physicochemical properties such as photoluminescence, photosensitizing ability for producing ROS, the ability to convert the absorbed light into heat, and the capacity to act as nanocarrier in drug delivery, make doped-CDs outstanding candidate to establish various multifunctional nanosystems in pharmaceutical and medical studies for enhanced treatment. From the biological point of view, doped-CDs can be easily incorporated into cells and enable cell labeling, cell imaging, and disease treatment. It is worth mentioning that to extend their practical in vivo application further studies should be focused on the long-term biological toxicity of doped-CDs.

However, there are still some barriers that should be overcome for further use of CDs in various fields, especially biomedical applications. For example, the high QY and intensive and stable brightness are vital parameters that should be considered in bioimaging. It is difficult to tailor doped-CDs with unique photophysical properties such as PL QY, emission range, and lifetime since the PL mechanism of doped-CDs and exact role and location of dopants in CDs are not yet completely understood. Therefore, for better understanding and controlling the various properties of doped-CDs, these aspects should be studied in the near future. The other remained obstacle is developing convenient strategies for achieving doped-CDs with uniform size distribution and high reproducibility. The size of doped-CDs plays an important role in controlling the toxicity, QY, and emission properties. As well, the uniformity of doped-CD size is vital for in vivo applications. The reproducibility of CDs is also an important issue, particularly for clinical applications.

It is worth mentioning that a wide variety of synthesis methods and pre-sources have been reported for the preparation of different CDs after the first report in 2004. However, the development of new CDs, without improved properties and applications compared to previous CDs, seems to be not attractive and valuable. Therefore, future efforts should focus on the systematic and controllable synthesis of CDs with novel specific properties and applications. For example, doping CDs with elements such as Gd and I, as MRI and CT imaging reagents have shown to produce nontoxic MRI and CT reagents with improved imaging contrast. Also, doping CDs with Al, as a confirmed adjuvant, resulted in CDs that could be applied as safe and suitable adjuvant carriers (against undoped CDs). As another example, introducing some transition-metal elements such as Cu, Fe, etc. into CDs could improve the applicability of CDs in photodynamic and photothermal therapy.

The selective application of doped-CDs based on the relationship between structure, function, and application is still on demand and should be addressed in the future. To expand the applicability of the nanosensors based on doped-CDs in the diagnosis fields, researchers should try to establish the sensitive and specific nanosensors by tailoring doped-CDs that can specifically interact with the analyte.

It should be mentioned that despite the outstanding properties of doped-CDs such as biocompatibility, low toxicity and renal clearance, their application in therapy is limited; thus efforts should be made by scientists to apply doped-CDs in different fields of diseases treatment, in vivo imaging, and both drug and gene delivery for better understanding of the cons and pros of this type of nanoparticles.

Concerning these cases, still more studies should focus on identifying novel sources and controllable techniques for the synthesis of doped-CDs with desired properties. In the light of these developments, the researchers could simply choose a promising synthesis strategy according to the specific application they are considering. It seems that by passing the mentioned obstacles within a few next years, doped-CDs can be turned to promising nanomaterials for different nanomedical applications.
